description: README.md is significantly out of date. Says six tutorial chapters complete (there are 13), roadmap tables show kernel primitives and fc.py features as not-started when most are done, references getting-started/ directory which is actually docs/, and the status section doesn't reflect current project state.

The primitives table stops at the early set (DUP, DROP, SWAP, OVER, +, -, @, !, EMIT, CR, HALT). Missing all primitives added for chapters 6-12: DO, LOOP, I, *, /MOD, KEY, 0BRANCH, BRANCH, =, <>, <, >, 0=, AT. Memory layout also shows an old START address.

summary: forth/tools/README.md shows completed features as not-yet
description: The 'What fc.py does not do' table still lists IF/THEN/LOOP as not yet implemented — they are done. The compilation pipeline description omits DO/LOOP, IF/ELSE/THEN, BEGIN/AGAIN/UNTIL. Uses CLOADM which should be LOADM.

summary: Bare Naked kernel: replace POLCAT with direct PIA0 keyboard scan
description: CODE_KEY (line 350 of forth/kernel/kernel.asm) is the kernel's only ROM dependency, calling JSR [$A000] (POLCAT via Extended Color BASIC ROM). Replacing this with direct PIA0 access makes the kernel fully ROM-independent and ready for cartridge delivery without BASIC loaded. Work includes: (1) new KBD-SCAN primitive writing col_mask to PIA0-B ($FF02) and reading PIA0-A ($FF00); (2) rewritten CODE_KEY with register-based debounce and MATRIX2ASCII lookup table; (3) AND and OR primitives; (4) KEY-HELD? Forth word; (5) optional KEY? non-blocking variant. Also requires fc.py word table updates for kbd-scan, and, or.

Add CODE_AND ( n1 n2 -- n ) and CODE_OR ( n1 n2 -- n ) to kernel.asm (CFA table entries + implementations). Add 'and' and 'or' to fc.py kernel_words dict. These are needed by KEY-HELD? and general bitwise application code.

summary: PIA0-A direction register init in START
description: Add bare-metal PIA0 initialisation to the START routine in kernel.asm: write to PIA0-A control register ($FF01) to set port A as input and enable the data register, and write to PIA0-B control register ($FF03) to set port B as output. BASIC does this at boot; a bare-metal cartridge must do it explicitly. Prerequisite for KBD-SCAN and the new CODE_KEY.

Add CODE_KBD_SCAN to kernel.asm: write col_mask to PIA0-B ($FF02), read PIA0-A ($FF00), invert, mask bit 7 (joystick comparator), push result. Passing $00 strobes all columns simultaneously for a fast any-key check. Add 'kbd-scan' to fc.py kernel_words. Depends on issue 6 (PIA0 init).

Replace CODE_KEY's JSR [$A000] / POLCAT call with a direct PIA0 scan loop. Includes: (1) register-based two-poll debounce (no RAM needed); (2) MATRIX2ASCII BSR subroutine with a 56-entry lookup table (7 cols x 8 rows) mapping matrix position to ASCII; (3) conditional assembly (IFDEF COCO3) for CoCo 3 variant table covering F1/F2/ALT. Eliminates the last ROM dependency. Depends on issues 6 and 7.

Implement INCLUDE <filename> as a compile-time host directive in fc.py. When the tokeniser encounters INCLUDE, it reads the named file (relative to the source file's directory) and splices its tokens into the stream before continuing. Enables a lib/ directory of shared Forth source. Standard ANS Forth FILE word set behaviour, implemented as a cross-compiler host operation.

summary: KEY-HELD? word and KB-Cx constants in lib/keyboard.fs
description: Create forth/lib/keyboard.fs containing: column mask constants (KB-C0 through KB-C6) as Forth CONSTANT definitions, and KEY-HELD? ( col_mask row_bit -- flag ) defined in pure Forth as: SWAP KBD-SCAN AND. Depends on issues 5 (AND), 7 (KBD-SCAN), and 9 (INCLUDE). Applications include it with: INCLUDE lib/keyboard.fs

Add CODE_KEY_NB to kernel.asm: same PIA0 scan as CODE_KEY but returns 0 immediately if no key is pressed rather than spinning. Useful for game loops, timeouts, and polling. Add 'key?' to fc.py kernel_words. Depends on issue 8 (MATRIX2ASCII table, which KEY? will share via BSR).

Create src/typewriter/typewriter.fs: a simple app that calls KEY in a loop and EMITs each character to the screen. Tests the new PIA0-based KEY implementation end-to-end. ENTER advances to the next row (CR), CLEAR resets cursor to top-left. Includes cursor display, arrow key movement, backspace, SHIFT support, and VSYNC-based auto-repeat.

Classic snake game using VDG semigraphics-4 color blocks. Arrow keys control direction, snake grows when eating food, game over on wall/self collision. Exercises keyboard input, game loop timing (VSYNC), screen drawing, and game state management.

Arrow keys move a brush cursor, number keys select colors. Uses VDG semigraphics modes for a 64x32 color pixel canvas. Demonstrates semigraphics block characters, color selection, and interactive screen painting.
Explore the CoCo's 6-bit DAC on PIA1. Simple tone generator or melody player. Could play a sequence of notes or allow interactive keyboard-to-tone mapping (piano). Opens up PIA1 hardware access.
Cellular automaton on the VDG semigraphics grid. Visually satisfying and a good stress test for Forth performance on the 6809. Could use semigraphics-4 for color or text mode for simplicity.
Stars flying toward the viewer, simulating depth with different speeds. Pure VDG semigraphics, no math library needed. VSYNC-timed animation loop.
Big digit display using block characters, ticks off the VSYNC 60Hz counter. Could include a stopwatch mode with start/stop/reset. Demonstrates timed display updates and large character rendering.

Random recursive backtracker draws maze walls in real time using semigraphics, then lets the player navigate with arrow keys. Requires a random number generator (could seed from VSYNC timing or keyboard).

Navigate between rooms with N/S/E/W commands. Room descriptions, items to pick up, simple puzzles. Pushes the Forth toolchain into string handling, data tables for room connections, and state tracking. A CoCo classic.
Walking character or bouncing ball using VDG semigraphics. Explores smooth movement, erase-draw cycles, and VSYNC-timed animation on the CoCo's character-mapped display.

Hex dump display, live memory editor, peek/poke any address. Arrow keys navigate, hex input to modify bytes. Actually useful as a development tool for inspecting kernel state, variables, and screen RAM.
Wait for a random delay, flash the screen, measure how fast the player hits a key. Uses VSYNC frame counter for timing. Simple but fun, and demonstrates precise timing measurement.

Hook up the CoCo's serial port (PIA1 bit-banged or cartridge slot UART) and make a dumb terminal. Most ambitious hardware-wise — connects the CoCo to the outside world. Practical for DriveWire or other serial communication.

Disassemble memory regions into 6809 mnemonics. Takes a start address and length, displays address, hex bytes, and decoded instruction. Must handle variable-length instructions (1-5 bytes), $10/$11 prefix pages, and indexed addressing postbyte decoding. Big but highly useful for inspecting compiled Forth output and kernel code on-device.

Display data stack and return stack contents on screen. Show stack depth and values in hex. Small enough to be a library word — no kernel changes needed. Immediately useful for debugging any Forth program. Could be invoked with a word like .S (print stack).

Show all compiled words with their CFA addresses. Helps verify that fc.py produced what you expected. Could display kernel primitives and app-defined words with their entry points. Requires fc.py to emit a word table or symbol list into the binary.

A Forth word (e.g. BRK or DEBUG) that can be inserted anywhere in Forth code. When executed, dumps registers and stack contents to the screen and waits for a keypress before continuing. Like a printf-debugger for Forth. Implementable as a library word using existing primitives.

Diff two memory regions byte-by-byte, highlighting differences. Takes two addresses and a length. Useful for verifying binaries match expectations or checking if RAM has been corrupted. Display shows address, expected byte, actual byte for each mismatch.

Single-step through the Forth thread, showing each word as it executes with stack state. The most powerful debugging tool but also the hardest — requires hooking DOCOL and NEXT in the kernel to intercept each threaded dispatch. Could show CFA address, word name (if symbol table available), and stack contents at each step.
